---
title: "M05: Visualizing Data with Tables"
author: "Michelle Diaz"
date: today
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
    theme: cosmo
    code-fold: false
    code-tools: true
    embed-resources: true
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
---

# Reflection on Visualizing Data with Tables

## What I Learned From the Videos

> Summarize what you learned from the videos you watched.

From the videos, I learned that `gt` is an R package that gives users the ability to make tables look clear, concise, and visually appealing while remaining easy to read. The name `gt` stands for "grammar of tables," referring to the different elements of a table that you can customize. I learned that there are many ways to turn raw data into a polished table, including adding a title and subtitle with `tab_header()`, aligning the header with `opt_align_table_header()`, creating a table stub with `rowname_col()`, customizing column labels with `cols_label()`, formatting numbers with `fmt_number()`, adjusting column widths with `cols_width()`, adding source notes with `tab_source_note()` and footnotes with `tab_footnote()`, styling cells with `tab_style()`, labeling the stubhead with `tab_stubhead()`, changing fonts with `opt_table_font()`, and automatically scaling cell color based on value with `data_color()`. I also learned that `gtExtras` is an extension of the `gt` package that adds more features and saves time. Overall, gt tables are accessible, easily modifiable, and reproducible.

## What I Liked About gt and gtExtras

> What did you like about the gt and gtExtras packages demonstrated in the videos?

I liked that `gtExtras` gives you the ability to add icons to a table, which helps explain the data more clearly at a glance. With `gt`, I liked the ability to assign color to specific values, such as the example in the video where you can color only the negative values, which makes important information stand out immediately. I also appreciated how much these packages let you combine textual data with small plots in a single table, making the information both readable and visually engaging.

## How Tables Complement Charts

> How do tables complement charts for data visualization?

Tables complement charts because the two serve different purposes. Charts are better for big-picture visualization, showing trends, patterns, and overall shape at a glance. Tables, on the other hand, are great for showcasing specific numerical values across multiple categories or sections, giving the reader the exact figures. Used together, they provide both the broad visual story and the precise underlying detail.

## When I Would Prefer Tables Over Charts

> Under what circumstances would you prefer to use tables rather than charts to visualize data?

I would prefer to use a table rather than a chart when I need to showcase specific numerical values across multiple categories or sections, especially when the exact numbers matter more than the overall visual pattern. Tables are ideal when precision is important, such as comparing detailed figures side by side, or when a reader needs to look up a specific value rather than just grasp a general trend.

# Building a Table With gt and gtExtras

## Setup

```{r}
#| label: setup

# Run once if needed (commented so the document renders):
# install.packages("gt")
# install.packages("gtExtras")

library(tidyverse)
library(gt)
library(gtExtras)
```

## Generate the Synthetic Data

```{r}
#| label: data

set.seed(123)
n <- 3000

mail_data <- tibble(
  customer_id = paste0("C", str_pad(1:n, 5, pad = "0")),
  age = round(rnorm(n, mean = 45, sd = 12)),
  income = round(rlnorm(n, meanlog = 10.8, sdlog = 0.6)),
  recency_days = round(rexp(n, rate = 1 / 60)),
  freq_12mo = rpois(n, lambda = 3),
  avg_order_amt = round(rlnorm(n, meanlog = 4.2, sdlog = 0.5), 2),
  channel = sample(
    c("email", "direct_mail", "digital"),
    n, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.5, 0.3, 0.2)
  ),
  region = sample(c("West", "South", "Midwest", "Northeast"), n, replace = TRUE),
  loyalty_tier = sample(
    c("Bronze", "Silver", "Gold", "Platinum"),
    n, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.4, 0.3, 0.2, 0.1)
  )
) |>
  mutate(
    income = if_else(runif(n) < 0.08, NA_real_, income),
    avg_order_amt = if_else(runif(n) < 0.05, NA_real_, avg_order_amt)
  )

glimpse(mail_data)
```

## Wrangle: Summarize by Channel

Before piping into `gt`, the data is summarized by marketing channel.

```{r}
#| label: wrangle

channel_summary <- mail_data |>
  group_by(channel) |>
  summarise(
    n_customers = n(),
    avg_age = mean(age, na.rm = TRUE),
    avg_income = mean(income, na.rm = TRUE),
    avg_order = mean(avg_order_amt, na.rm = TRUE),
    avg_freq = mean(freq_12mo, na.rm = TRUE)
  )

channel_summary
```

## The Final Table

```{r}
#| label: gt-table

channel_summary |>
  gt(rowname_col = "channel") |>
  tab_header(
    title = "Customer Profile by Marketing Channel",
    subtitle = "Average customer metrics across email, direct mail, and digital"
  ) |>
  cols_label(
    n_customers = "Customers",
    avg_age = "Avg. Age",
    avg_income = "Avg. Income",
    avg_order = "Avg. Order",
    avg_freq = "Avg. Purchases"
  ) |>
  fmt_currency(columns = c(avg_income, avg_order), decimals = 2) |>
  fmt_number(columns = c(avg_age, avg_freq), decimals = 2) |>
  tab_spanner(
    label = "Customer Value Metrics",
    columns = c(avg_income, avg_order, avg_freq)
  ) |>
  tab_stubhead(label = "Channel") |>
  tab_source_note(
    source_note = "Source: Synthetic direct-mail marketing dataset (n = 3,000)."
  ) |>
  tab_footnote(
    footnote = "Income figures exclude missing values (approximately 8% of records).",
    locations = cells_column_labels(columns = avg_income)
  ) |>
  data_color(columns = avg_income, palette = c("white", "#005030")) |>
  gt_theme_538()
```

# Why This Table Is Worth Adding to My MSDM Project

> Explain why the table is worth adding to your MSDM project written report or presentation slides.

Adding a table like this to an MSDM project is very useful because it clearly displays the data in a format that is readable and digestible. Rather than only using a graph, a well-designed table breaks the data down and presents it in a clean, organized way that makes the numbers easy to interpret at a glance. It is also a valuable skill to develop because it makes working with data seamless, since everything happens in one application instead of having to transfer the data into another program like Excel, Canva, or Google Sheets to build a table. On top of saving time, it keeps the work reproducible and adds a layer of professionalism to coursework and to any client-facing report or presentation.

# Appendix


**GitHub Repository:** [ADD YOUR REPO URL HERE](ADD-YOUR-REPO-URL-HERE)

**GitHub Pages (Published Report):** [ADD YOUR PAGES URL HERE](ADD-YOUR-PAGES-URL-HERE)
